11: ki nefesh habasar badam hi vaani netatihu lachem al hamizbeach lekapper al nafshoteichem ki hadam hu bakafra banefesh: 'For the life [nefesh] of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls [nefesh], for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life.' The blood-for-life exchange is the theological axiom underlying all sacrifice: blood (= life) offered for life (= the offerer's forfeit life). The principle is stated as divine appointment — 'I have given it for you on the altar' — sacrifice is not a human invention but a divinely ordained mechanism of atonement. Hebrews 9:22's 'without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins' is a direct application of Lev 17:11 to Christ's death.
1: Standard address formula. Chapter 17 opens the Holiness Code's initial layer — the blood laws that regulate all animal slaughter and consumption. The broad addressee (Aaron, sons, all Israel) signals a universal obligation extending beyond the sanctuary to all Israelite life.
2: The law extends to 'all the people of Israel' (kol-bnei Yisrael) — not merely the priests — and specifically names the act of slaughtering (yishḥat) livestock. The law governs not just ritual sacrifice but ordinary butchering: in the wilderness period, no animal may be slaughtered outside the sanctuary.
3: ish ish mibbeit Yisrael asher yishḥat: 'any man of Israel who slaughters.' The repeated ish ish (man, man = anyone/whoever) is the legal universalizing formula in Leviticus. The three animals: shor (ox/bull), keves (lamb), ez (goat) — the domesticated animals that can be offered sacrificially. The rule applies regardless of location: inside or outside the camp.
4: vedam yiḥasheiv laish hahu dam shafakh: 'blood shall be reckoned to that man — he has shed blood.' Anyone who slaughters without bringing the animal to the sanctuary entrance is guilty of shefikh dam (bloodshed) — treated like murder. This is because unauthorized slaughter severs the connection between killing and atonement that the sanctuary system maintains. Venikh'rat haish hahu miqqerev ammo: 'that man shall be cut off from among his people' — the karet penalty, which represents the most severe divine punishment short of execution.
5: The purpose of centralizing slaughter at the sanctuary: lema'an asher yavi'u bnei Yisrael et-ziḥeihem (so that the Israelites may bring their sacrifices). The word ziḥeihem (their sacrifices, from zevaḥ) refers to what they were previously offering in the open field — slaughter-meals that had a quasi-sacrificial character. By requiring all such slaughter to come to the sanctuary, YHWH absorbs and Torahizes the universal human impulse to mark killing with ritual.
6: The priest throws (zaraq) the blood against the altar — the verb is distinctive, suggesting a vigorous throwing action rather than careful application. The fat is burned as a reiaḥ niḥoaḥ (pleasing aroma). By requiring all slaughter to flow through this mechanism, every meat meal in Israel becomes a form of communion with YHWH.
7: velo-yizbeḥu od et-ziḥeihem lasse'irim asher hem zonim aḥarei-hem: 'They shall no longer sacrifice their offerings to the goat-demons (se'irim) after whom they have been whoring.' Se'irim (goat-demons, sg. sa'ir) — the term for the semi-divine wilderness beings associated with arid places and disorder (Isa 13:21, 34:14). The use of zonim (whoring/prostituting) for idolatry is the standard prophetic language for covenant unfaithfulness. The law addresses a real syncretistic practice in Israel's past.
8: The law extends to resident aliens (ger) — the foreigner living among Israel is subject to the same slaughter centralization rule. This is a recurring pattern in the Holiness Code: the resident alien is included in Israel's covenant obligations, not just Israel's covenant privileges. Ger haggur betokham (the alien who sojourns among them) cannot maintain a parallel pagan sacrificial system.
9: The penalty for unauthorized sacrifice (even of permitted animals) is karet — being cut off from the people. This applies equally to native Israelite and resident alien. The equivalence of native and alien under the blood laws reflects the Holiness Code's consistent expansion of covenant obligations beyond ethnic Israel.
10: veish ish mibbeit Yisrael umin-hager hagar betokham asher yokhal kol-dam: 'any man of Israel or resident alien who eats any blood.' The blood prohibition is broader than the slaughter law: it applies to any blood in any form. The penalty: unatati fanai banaefesh ha'okhelat et-hadam (I will set my face against the person who eats blood and cut him off) — YHWH's own personal attention is the enforcement mechanism. This is the most direct form of divine sanction in Leviticus, reserved for fundamental violations.
12: Summary restatement of the blood prohibition with the same universalizing ish ish formula. The double prohibition (no native, no alien) emphasizes that the blood taboo transcends ethnic and covenant boundaries — it reflects something about blood's intrinsic association with life that makes consumption an offense against the life-principle itself.
13: asher yatsud tseid ḥayyah o-oph asher ye'akhel: 'who hunts a game animal or bird that may be eaten.' Game animals (hunted rather than domesticated) are subject to a special blood rule: since their blood cannot be brought to the altar (they are not slaughter animals), it must be poured out and covered with earth (ve'ikissahu be'afar). Shafakh dam (poured out) + kissa (covered) — the blood is given back to the earth, acknowledging that it belongs to YHWH even when it cannot go to the altar.
14: ki nefesh kol-basar damo venafshoto hu: 'the life of every creature — its blood is its life.' Restatement of the v. 11 principle applied to game animals: the blood-prohibition follows from the blood-life equation. The phrase kol-asher yokhlenu yikkareth (whoever eats it shall be cut off) applies the karet penalty uniformly across all animal categories.
15: vekhol-nefesh asher tokhal nevelah uterefa ba'eziret uvagger: 'Every person — native or alien — who eats an animal that died naturally or was torn.' Nevelah (naturally dead) and terefa (torn by wild animals) — the two categories of non-slaughtered death. Eating them generates ritual impurity requiring clothes-washing and bathing; the person remains unclean until evening. This is a graduated penalty compared to the karet of v. 14 — eating from natural death is less severe than eating blood, but still requires purification.
16: ve'im lo yekhabbeis veguffo lo yirḥats venasa avono: 'if he does not wash his clothes and does not bathe his body, he shall bear his iniquity.' The responsibility for purification lies with the individual: failure to perform the ritual washing does not remove the impurity but makes the person culpable for remaining in an impure state. Venasa avono (he shall bear his iniquity) — the language of personal moral accountability applied to a ritual-purity context.
